masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Across the States
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Across the States
masthead.dek: Seven questions, seven different ways of measuring a state — latitude, size, height, age, people, lakes, lines on a map. Narrow the fifty down, then place your bet. Tap to check yourself.
masthead.footer_html: Seven questions · seven distinct dimensions (100% unique) · sources noted per question.

questions.q: Of all the state capitals in the connected 48 states, which one sits the farthest north?
questions.detail: About 47.0°N — the northernmost capital in the contiguous U.S.
questions.why: It edges out the capitals people picture as 'up north': roughly 0.2° north of Bismarck, North Dakota (46.8°N) and well north of Helena, Montana (46.6°N).
questions.more: Most guesses go to Bismarck, Helena, or Augusta, Maine (44.3°N) — but the Pacific Northwest takes it. Juneau, Alaska (58°N) is the northernmost of all 50.
questions.source: U.S. extreme-points data; city coordinates (Olympia 47.04°N, Bismarck 46.81°N).

questions.q: Three states are bigger than this one, but every one of those three touches an ocean. What is the largest state with no ocean coast?
questions.detail: About 147,040 sq mi — 4th-largest state, largest with no saltwater coastline.
questions.why: Only Alaska, Texas, and California are larger, and each meets the sea. Montana beats the next inland giant, New Mexico (~121,600 sq mi), by more than 25,000 sq mi.
questions.more: A common wrong guess is Texas, but its Gulf coast disqualifies it. Among states truly cut off from the ocean, Montana clears Nevada, Arizona, and Colorado comfortably.
questions.source: U.S. Census Bureau state area figures.

questions.q: Every state has a single highest point. In one state, that highest point is lower than the highest point of any other state. Which state is it?
questions.detail: Its high point, Britton Hill, tops out at just 345 feet.
questions.why: Florida's tallest natural ground is the shortest 'peak' in the country — 345 ft, more than 100 ft below the next-lowest, Delaware's Ebright Azimuth (448 ft).
questions.more: The fair finalists are all flat coastal states — Delaware, Louisiana, Mississippi, Rhode Island — but Florida's summit wins by a clear margin. Several Florida buildings stand taller than Britton Hill.
questions.source: USGS / state high-point elevations (Florida 345 ft; Delaware 448 ft).

questions.q: Setting aside Alaska and Hawaii, which state was the last to join the United States?
questions.why: It barely earned last place: New Mexico joined the same year but five weeks earlier (January 6, 1912), leaving Arizona as the final piece of the lower 48.
questions.more: The other late arrivals were Oklahoma (1907) and New Mexico (1912). Alaska and Hawaii both came much later, in 1959.
questions.source: Library of Congress / U.S. statehood records (Arizona admitted Feb 14, 1912).

questions.q: Which state has the fewest people living in it?
questions.detail: About 580,000 residents — the least populous state.
questions.why: Despite ranking 10th-largest by area, it holds fewer people than the city of Milwaukee — roughly 580,000 at the 2020 census.
questions.more: Vermont (~643,000) and Alaska (~733,000) are the next-fewest, so a couple of small states stay in play before you settle on Wyoming. (As of the 2020 census.)

questions.q: Several states sit on the Great Lakes. Which state touches the most of the five?
questions.detail: It borders four of the five Great Lakes — all but Lake Ontario.
questions.why: No other state touches more than two, so Michigan doubles the field: its two peninsulas put it on Superior, Michigan, Huron, and Erie.
questions.more: New York is one of the few states on two lakes (Erie and Ontario). The only Great Lake Michigan misses is Ontario.
questions.source: Standard U.S. geography references (Michigan borders 4 of 5 Great Lakes).

questions.q: Which state is divided into the fewest counties?
questions.detail: Just 3 counties — Kent, New Castle, and Sussex.
questions.why: Nothing is close at the bottom: the next-fewest states have five each, so Delaware runs an entire state on about a third fewer top-level divisions than anyone else.
questions.more: Hawaii and Rhode Island come next at 5 (Rhode Island's have no county government at all). Louisiana uses parishes and Alaska uses boroughs instead of counties.
